metadata.seed: I don’t want to be a burden on other people. So I never share anything and pretend I’m fine and smile.

patient.query: I don’t want to be a burden on other people. So I never share anything and pretend I’m fine and smile.

automatic_thoughts: I don’t want to be a burden on other people
I have to pretend I’m fine

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
neutral: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of worry that you might be a burden. What evidence do you have that others feel burdened by you, and can you think of moments when people have shared with you or offered help? How might your relationships change if you let someone know how you’re really feeling?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of being a burden to others
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: I’ve been feeling really overwhelmed lately. It’s like I can’t keep up with everything, and I just want to crawl into bed and hide. There are moments where I feel so anxious, and then I just freeze, unable to do anything. I keep thinking maybe I should be doing better, and that just makes it worse.

automatic_thoughts: I should be doing better

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure to keep up is weighing heavily on you, and that can feel exhausting. What evidence do you have that you’re not meeting that standard, and are there moments when you’ve handled things well? You might find it helpful to consider a more balanced view of what “doing better” looks like in this moment.

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel heavy. I guess I just keep thinking about all the things I didn’t do or messed up. Sometimes I do manage to handle things well, but those moments feel so small compared to the bigger picture. I often focus on what’s going wrong instead of what’s going right. It’s frustrating because I want to be better, but I feel like I’m just stuck.

automatic_thoughts: thinking about all the things I didn’t do or messed up
moments feel so small compared to the bigger picture
focus on what’s going wrong instead of what’s going right
I want to be better, but I feel like I’m just stuck

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck because you keep focusing on what you didn’t do. What evidence do you have that you’re truly stuck, and can you think of any moments that show you’re actually making progress? Exploring both sides might help you see a clearer picture of where you are.

patient.query: I mean, I do have those moments where I feel like I’ve accomplished something, even if they’re small. Like, I managed to finish a project at work last week, and I was proud of that. But then I look at everything else I haven’t done, and it just feels like it overshadows the small wins. It’s like no matter how hard I try, I keep

triggers: looking at everything else I haven’t done
automatic_thoughts: it overshadows the small wins
no matter how hard I try, I keep

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel your small wins are eclipsed by everything else you haven’t done. What evidence supports that idea, and what evidence might suggest those wins still matter?

patient.query: I guess I just see all the big tasks piling up, and it’s hard to feel like those little things count for much. It’s like they’re dropped in a bucket with a huge hole. And sure, I can think of times when others have acknowledged my efforts, but in my mind, it just doesn’t feel enough. Sometimes I feel like I’m just not doing enough, you know

automatic_thoughts: little things don't count
my efforts aren't enough
I'm not doing enough

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that the small steps you take are truly insignificant? Can you recall a moment when a seemingly minor effort made a difference for you or someone else? How might you reframe those moments to see them as meaningful progress rather than just “not enough”?

patient.query: I mean, it's hard to see them as significant when I’m wrapped up in my own head. I know there have been times when I helped a friend with something small, and they really appreciated it, but my mind just brushes it off like it doesn’t matter. I guess in those moments, I convince myself that I should be doing more. It’s like I can’t connect those little actions

automatic_thoughts: I should be doing more
those little actions don't matter

entailment: Pays attention to own choice of activity (finding)
neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that those small acts don’t matter? Can you think of a time when your help made a difference to someone? How might you view those moments if you considered the value they actually had?

patient.query: I suppose I remember a time when I helped a coworker with a report they were really stressed about, and they seemed relieved afterwards. They thanked me, and I thought, “That’s nice,” but then just moved on. It’s like I dismissed it, thinking it was no big deal. If I really sat with that, maybe I could see it as something more, but my mind just

triggers: helped a coworker with a report
automatic_thoughts: that’s nice
it was no big deal

llm_response.response: It sounds like you noticed a moment of appreciation but then let it slip away. What might be going on when you decide to move on so quickly? How do you feel about the idea that your help mattered to someone else?

patient.query: I guess I feel a bit... uncomfortable thinking about it too much. Like, if I acknowledge that my help actually made a difference, then I’d have to face the fact that I can do good things, and that feels risky. What if it was just a fluke? I don’t want to overthink it or get my hopes up. So maybe it’s easier to just push those moments

automatic_thoughts: if I acknowledge that my help actually made a difference, then I’d have to face the fact that I can do good things
what if it was just a fluke?
I don’t want to overthink it or get my hopes up

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that your help actually made a difference? How might you view that impact without feeling it has to change your sense of self? What would happen if you considered that it could be more than a fluke?

patient.query: I mean, I know my coworker seemed really grateful, and they said it helped lighten their load. That part sticks with me, I guess. But then there’s this voice in my head that just says it was nothing. If I let myself think it meant anything, what if I start expecting that from myself all the time? It feels safer to just brush it off and not put myself out

triggers: they said it helped lighten their load
automatic_thoughts: it was nothing
what if I start expecting that from myself all the time

neutral: Normal grief reaction to life event (finding)

llm_response.response: It’s understandable to feel anxious about the idea of being expected to keep helping, especially if it feels like you might not be wanted. What evidence do you have that if you value your help, you will be expected to keep doing it? It might be useful to think about times when you felt pressured versus times when you felt free to choose.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I’m just worried that if I show I care or that I’m good at something, people will start relying on me. And that feels like a lot of pressure. I remember times when I felt like I had to step up, and it was exhausting. But… there are also times when I chose to jump in because it felt right. I just don’t know how to

automatic_thoughts: if I show I care, people will start relying on me
that feels like a lot of pressure
I had to step up, and it was exhausting

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that showing care will inevitably make people rely on you? Can you think of times when you cared or helped and it didn’t lead to that kind of pressure? How might you experiment with setting small boundaries to see if the outcome is different?
